title: AD Playbook — Users, Computers & Logged-On Sessions
body: This is the practical AD relationship playbook: enumerate users, groups, computers, local admin access, and where users are logged in. The goal is to discover paths like: current user has admin on a workstation where a more privileged user has a session.
cmd:
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# AD PLAYBOOK — USERS, COMPUTERS, LOGGED-ON USERS
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Authorized lab / assessment use only.
# Start from a domain-joined Windows client with low-privileged creds.

# ── 0) SESSION CONTEXT ────────────────────────────
whoami
whoami /all
hostname
ipconfig /all
echo %USERDOMAIN%
echo %LOGONSERVER%

# Record:
# - current user
# - current computer
# - domain
# - DC / DNS server
# - current privileges

# ── 1) LEGACY WINDOWS ENUM — FAST BASELINE ────────
net user /domain
net group /domain
net group "Domain Admins" /domain
net accounts /domain
net view /domain
nltest /dclist:corp.com
nltest /domain_trusts

# Specific user and group examples.
net user stephanie /domain
net user jeffadmin /domain
net group "Management Department" /domain
net group "Sales Department" /domain

# Note: net.exe is fast but limited.
# It can miss nested groups, so confirm with PowerView.

# ── 2) IMPORT POWERVIEW ───────────────────────────
powershell -ep bypass
cd C:\Tools
Import-Module .\PowerView.ps1

# ── 3) USERS — WHO EXISTS AND WHO MATTERS ─────────
Get-DomainUser |
  select samaccountname,cn,description,pwdlastset,lastlogon

# Passwords or clues in description.
Get-DomainUser -Properties samaccountname,description |
  ? {$_.description} |
  select samaccountname,description

# High-value / protected users.
Get-DomainUser -AdminCount |
  select samaccountname,memberof,admincount

# Domain Admins and nested membership.
Get-DomainGroupMember "Domain Admins" -Recurse

# ── 4) GROUPS — MAP NESTED RELATIONSHIPS ──────────
Get-DomainGroup | select cn

# Look at custom groups one by one.
Get-DomainGroup "Sales Department" | select member
Get-DomainGroup "Development Department" | select member
Get-DomainGroup "Management Department" | select member

# Recursive membership.
Get-DomainGroupMember "Sales Department" -Recurse
Get-DomainGroupMember "Development Department" -Recurse
Get-DomainGroupMember "Management Department" -Recurse

# Why this matters:
# A user may inherit access through group nesting even when net.exe output looks harmless.

# ── 5) COMPUTERS — BUILD TARGET INVENTORY ─────────
Get-DomainComputer |
  select dnshostname,operatingsystem,operatingsystemversion

# Save a computer list for repeat checks.
Get-DomainComputer |
  select -ExpandProperty dnshostname |
  Out-File .\computers.txt

type .\computers.txt

# ── 6) LOCAL ADMIN ACCESS — WHERE CAN I ADMIN? ────
Find-LocalAdminAccess

# If it returns a host, that host becomes high-priority.
# Example:
# CLIENT74.corp.com
#
# Meaning:
# current user can likely control services/files/processes on that host.

# ── 7) SESSION ENUM WITH POWERVIEW ────────────────
# These may fail on newer systems. Always use -Verbose if blank.
Get-NetSession -ComputerName files04
Get-NetSession -ComputerName files04 -Verbose

Get-NetSession -ComputerName web04
Get-NetSession -ComputerName web04 -Verbose

Get-NetSession -ComputerName client74
Get-NetSession -ComputerName client74 -Verbose

# Logged-on users through workstation APIs.
Get-NetLoggedon -ComputerName client74
Get-NetLoggedon -ComputerName client74 -Verbose

# Hunt specific high-value user.
Find-DomainUserLocation -UserName jeffadmin

# Hunt admin users and check whether you have access.
Invoke-UserHunter -CheckAccess

# ── 8) SESSION ENUM WITH PSLOGGEDON ───────────────
# PsLoggedOn uses registry/session techniques.
# It needs Remote Registry for local logon enumeration.
# Remote Registry is often disabled on workstations, but commonly available on servers.
cd C:\Tools\PSTools

.\PsLoggedon.exe \\files04
.\PsLoggedon.exe \\web04
.\PsLoggedon.exe \\client74

# Interpret results:
# Users logged on locally:
#   means an interactive/local logon may exist.
#
# Users logged on via resource shares:
#   means SMB/resource session information.
#
# Unable to query resource logons:
#   resource session query failed, but local logon output may still be useful.
#
# Access is denied:
#   you likely lack rights or the required service/path is unavailable.
#
# No one is logged on locally:
#   treat as useful, but not absolute proof.

# ── 9) COMBINE FINDINGS INTO ATTACK-PATH TABLE ───
# Build this table in notes:
#
# Computer        OS                  MyAccess      LoggedOnUsers       WhyImportant
# CLIENT74        Windows 11 Pro      LocalAdmin    CORP\jeffadmin      DA session target
# FILES04         Server 2022         Unknown       CORP\jeff           File server/user target
# WEB04           Server 2022         Unknown       None seen           Web/SPN target
#
# Strong path pattern:
# 1. I own / control user A.
# 2. User A has local admin on computer B.
# 3. Privileged user C has a session on computer B.
# 4. User C has high-value group membership or access.
#
# That is the path to validate in BloodHound and manually.

# ── 10) REPEAT AFTER EVERY PIVOT ──────────────────
# Every new user/computer gives a new viewpoint.
# Repeat:
whoami /all
net user /domain
Get-DomainUser | select samaccountname,description
Get-DomainGroupMember "Domain Admins" -Recurse
Get-DomainComputer | select dnshostname,operatingsystem
Find-LocalAdminAccess
Get-NetSession -ComputerName TARGET -Verbose
.\PsLoggedon.exe \\TARGET

# ── 11) DOCUMENTATION TEMPLATE ────────────────────
# Current user:
# Current computer:
# Domain:
# Local admin access found:
# Logged-on users found:
# High-value accounts:
# Interesting groups:
# Interesting computers:
# Possible paths:
# Manual validation:
# Next move:
warn: Blank session output does not always mean no users are logged in. Modern Windows permissions can block NetSessionEnum-style checks. Use -Verbose, try PsLoggedOn, check multiple machines, and cross-check with BloodHound. PsLoggedOn depends heavily on Remote Registry for local logon enumeration.

choices.label: Found local admin on another computer
